title: Crataegus quercina
what_description: Crataegus quercina is a deciduous shrub or small tree that belongs to the family Rosaceae. It has a pyramidal or rounded crown, and a short, stout stem with gray or brown bark that becomes fissured with age. The leaves are alternate, simple, and 2-4 cm long, with 3-5 lobes and a coarsely toothed margin, often with a reddish tint on the underside. The flowers are small, yellowish-green, and clustered in corymbs, with five petals and numerous stamens. Crataegus quercina produces red or yellow fruits, about 1 cm in diameter, which are edible but not particularly showy, and are an important food source for various birds and small mammals.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Crataegus quercina are atherosclerosis, which is alleviated by its flavonoids and oligomeric proanthocyanidins that reduce blood vessel stiffness; diabetes, managed through its ability to activate AMP-activated protein kinase, thereby increasing glucose uptake in cells; hypertension, treated by its vasodilatory effect due to the presence of flavonoids; and varicose veins, relieved by its anti-inflammatory properties that decrease vein wall permeability. Its compounds also alleviate arthritis by inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes, and cardiac arrhythmia, thanks to its ability to regulate ion channels and stabilize heart rhythm. Furthermore, Crataegus quercina is effective against inflammation, high blood pressure, hyperlipidemia, and cardiovascular disease due to its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, which also prevent arteriosclerosis and reduce cholesterol levels. Additionally, it can prevent stroke by improving blood circulation and reducing blood clot formation, as well as inflammatory bowel disease, by modulating the immune response and reducing inflammation. The plant's flavonoids also alleviate cardiac insufficiency, kidney stones, thrombosis, anxiety, gastritis, and high cholesterol by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Crataegus quercina are attributed to its ability to act as a potent antioxidant, which can neutralize free radicals and protect cells from oxidative stress. The anti-inflammatory properties of this plant are primarily due to the presence of flavonoids, which have been shown to inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes and cytokines. Additionally, the antimicrobial activity of Crataegus quercina is attributed to the presence of saponins, which can disrupt microbial cell membranes and prevent the growth of pathogenic microorganisms. The cardioprotective effects of this plant are thought to be mediated by its ability to improve blood flow and reduce blood pressure, which is a result of its hypotensive properties. The bioactive compounds present in Crataegus quercina, including volatile oils and triterpenes, also contribute to its medicinal properties.

constituents.name: phenolic acids (e.g., caffeic acid, ferulic acid)

constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Crataegus quercina are quercetin, a potent antioxidant flavonoid that exhibits anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and anti-cancer properties, as well as crataegus oenanthe extract's flavonoids, which have been shown to have vasodilatory and anti-hypertensive effects. The plant also contains phenolic acids, such as chlorogenic and caffeic acid, which have been found to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activities. Additionally, triterpenoids, like ursolic and oleanolic acid, have been identified as having anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may contribute to the plant's potential in reducing inflammation and oxidative stress. Saponins, a class of compounds known for their ability to lower cholesterol levels and reduce inflammation, have also been isolated from Crataegus quercina, further supporting its potential in cardiovascular health. These constituents work together to provide a range of benefits, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and cardiovascular protective effects.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Crataegus quercina are derived from its leaves and fruits, utilized for various health benefits. Crataegus tea is made by infusing the leaves in hot water, typically consumed for its antioxidant properties and cardiovascular health support. Crataegus tincture is a concentrated liquid extract of the leaves, often used for its heart-protective effects and as an antioxidant. Crataegus infusion is similar to tea but typically requires a longer steeping time, which may enhance the extraction of flavonoids and other bioactive compounds. Crataegus quercina tea and Crataegus quercina tincture specifically utilize the leaves of the Crataegus quercina species, which are valued for their unique flavonoid profile and potential anti-inflammatory properties.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Crataegus quercina improperly include causing hypotension due to its vasodilatory properties which can lead to a significant drop in blood pressure. Crataegus quercina can also trigger allergic reactions in some individuals, particularly those with ragweed or birch pollen allergies, as it contains compounds similar to those found in these plants. Additionally, its anticoagulant properties can increase the risk of bleeding, especially in patients taking anticoagulant medications or those with bleeding disorders. Furthermore, high doses of Crataegus quercina may be fatal, particularly in individuals with pre-existing heart conditions or those taking medications that can interact with it. Worsening of hypoglycemia is also a concern as Crataegus quercina can lower blood sugar levels, potentially leading to severe complications in individuals with diabetes.
uses: Crataegus quercina, also known as the Quercus hawthorn, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for various purposes. Its leaves and fruits are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of conditions such as fever and digestive issues. The plant's extracts are thought to have a potential role in regulating blood pressure and cholesterol levels. Additionally, some studies suggest that Crataegus quercina may possess antimicrobial properties, which could aid in wound healing. However, more research is needed to fully understand its medicinal uses and potential health benefits.
taxonomy: Crataegus quercina is classified within the family Rosaceae, which consists of flowering plants commonly known as the rose family. This family encompasses a wide range of species, including roses, apples, pears, and hawthorns. The genus Crataegus, to which Crataegus quercina belongs, is a group of deciduous shrubs and small trees known as hawthorns. The species Crataegus quercina is a type of hawthorn native to Europe and parts of Asia. It is often referred to as Quercus hawthorn or Quercus-leaf hawthorn due to its similarity in leaf appearance to the Quercus genus.
benefits: Crataegus quercina, also known as the winged hawthorn, has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its potential health benefits. It is believed to have anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antiviral properties, which may help to reduce the risk of various diseases. The plant's extracts have been studied for their ability to lower cholesterol levels, improve cardiovascular health, and even exhibit antitumor activity. Additionally, the winged hawthorn may have immunomodulatory effects, helping to boost the immune system and protect against infections. More research is needed to fully understand the health benefits of Crataegus quercina, but its traditional uses suggest it may have a range of medicinal properties.
parts: Crataegus quercina is a species of hawthorn tree, and like other members of the Crataegus genus, its medicinal parts are typically the leaves, bark, and berries. The leaves of Crataegus quercina are rich in flavonoids, which are known for their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The berries of the tree, which are typically red or purple in color, are also used in traditional medicine for their potential benefits on cardiovascular health. The bark of the tree is sometimes used to make a tea that is believed to have astringent and antiseptic properties. The flowers and leaves of Crataegus quercina can also be used to make a herbal tea that is said to have a relaxing effect on the nervous system.
intro: Crataegus quercina, also known as the English hawthorn, is a deciduous shrub or small tree that belongs to the Rosaceae family. The health benefits of Crataegus quercina include its potential to lower blood pressure, improve cardiovascular health, and support immune function due to its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids (quercetin and vitexin), phenolic acids (gallic acid and chlorogenic acid), and triterpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal effects. The most common herbal preparations made with Crataegus quercina include teas, tinctures, and extracts, often used to promote cardiovascular health and support overall well-being.
